description: NICE established. No formal threshold published; an informal ~£20k-£30k range was later inferred from early appraisal decisions.
NICE Guide to the Methods of Technology Appraisal formally states the £20,000-£30,000/QALY standard range.
End-of-life criteria introduced: committees could accept ICERs up to roughly £50,000/QALY for life-extending treatments in patients with short life expectancy.
Highly Specialised Technologies (HST) programme launched for ultra-rare conditions. No explicit numeric threshold at first - deliberative committee judgement only.
HST formalised an explicit £100,000/QALY threshold, scaling via a QALY-gain weighting (1x-3x) up to £300,000/QALY for treatments delivering large health gains (>30 QALYs).
source: PMC7387327; Source Health Economics (2019), 'The NICE HST Process'

description: Severity modifier replaces end-of-life criteria. Base £20k-£30k threshold formally retained, but a 1.2x/1.7x QALY weighting based on QALY shortfall raises the effective threshold to ~£30k-£36k.

Reporting indicates the HST upper threshold extension (up to £300,000/QALY for very large QALY gains) was reconfirmed around this date - may overlap with the 2017 policy rather than being a distinct new change; flagged here for completeness.
UK government confirms the standard threshold will rise to £25,000-£35,000/QALY as part of the UK-US pharmaceutical trade deal, effective April 2026. (Announcement date, not effective date.)
source: NICE news (Dec 2025), 'Changes to NICE's cost-effectiveness thresholds confirmed'

description: New standard threshold takes effect - the first change to the headline range in over 20 years.
